questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The children ___ playing in the park when it started to rain.'
questions.explanation: 'Children' is plural, so we use 'were' (not 'was'). 'The children were playing' is the past continuous tense, describing an ongoing action in the past. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ran ___ than anyone else in the race.'
questions.explanation: When comparing two things (she vs everyone else), we use the comparative form 'faster'. The superlative 'fastest' would need 'the fastest', and we use '-er' not 'more' for short adjectives. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Neither the teacher ___ the pupils knew the answer.'
questions.explanation: 'Neither' always pairs with 'nor': neither...nor. Similarly, 'either' pairs with 'or'. These are called correlative conjunctions. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'If I ___ taller, I could reach the top shelf.'
questions.explanation: In conditional sentences about imaginary situations, we use 'were' for all subjects: 'If I were taller...'. This is called the subjunctive mood. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The letter was ___ by the headteacher herself.'
questions.explanation: This is a passive voice sentence ('was ___'). The passive requires the past participle: 'was written'. 'Wrote' is the simple past, not the past participle. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The team, along with their coach, ___ arriving at noon.'
questions.explanation: The subject is 'The team' (singular). The phrase 'along with their coach' is extra information and doesn't change the subject. So we use 'is' for a singular subject. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Tom has ___ to the shop to buy some milk.'
questions.explanation: With 'has', we need the past participle: 'has gone'. 'Went' is the simple past (Tom went to the shop), but with 'has' we must use 'gone'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The old house, ___ had stood on the hill for centuries, was finally demolished.'
questions.explanation: 'Which' is the correct relative pronoun for things (the house). 'Who' and 'whom' are for people. 'What' doesn't introduce relative clauses like this. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Each of the students ___ required to bring a packed lunch.'
questions.explanation: 'Each' is always singular, even when followed by 'of the students'. So we use 'is': 'Each of the students is required...'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The news about the floods ___ very worrying.'
questions.explanation: 'News' looks plural but is actually a singular noun. We say 'the news is' not 'the news are'. Other examples: mathematics is, physics is. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'My brother and I ___ going to the cinema tonight.'
questions.explanation: 'My brother and I' is a plural subject (two people), so we use 'are'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ her homework before dinner every day.'
questions.explanation: With 'she' (third person singular, present tense), we use 'does'. 'Do' is for I/you/we/they. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The dog wagged ___ tail happily.'
questions.explanation: 'Its' (without an apostrophe) shows possession — the tail belonging to the dog. 'It's' means 'it is'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Please could you pass ___ the salt?'
questions.explanation: 'Me' is the object pronoun — it receives the action. 'I' is the subject pronoun. 'Pass me' is correct because 'me' is receiving the salt. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The cake ___ eaten before I arrived.'
questions.explanation: 'Had been eaten' is the past perfect passive. It shows the eating was completed before another past event (my arrival). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'You should ___ studied harder for the test.'
questions.explanation: 'Should have' is correct. A common mistake is writing 'should of' because it sounds similar when spoken quickly, but 'of' is not a verb. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'There are ___ apples in the bowl than there were yesterday.'
questions.explanation: 'Fewer' is used for countable nouns (apples, people, books). 'Less' is for uncountable nouns (water, time, money). Since apples can be counted, we use 'fewer'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The children played ___ in the garden all afternoon.'
questions.explanation: We need an adverb to describe HOW they played. 'Happily' is the adverb form of 'happy'. Adverbs modify verbs (played), while adjectives modify nouns. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ going to rain this afternoon, so bring an umbrella.'
questions.explanation: 'It's' is short for 'it is'. 'It is going to rain' → 'It's going to rain'. 'Its' (no apostrophe) shows possession. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The prize was awarded to my sister and ___.'
questions.explanation: 'Me' is correct because it's the object of the verb 'awarded'. A good test: remove 'my sister and' — you would say 'The prize was awarded to me' not 'to I'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Neither the teacher ___ the students were happy about the cancelled trip.'
questions.explanation: 'Neither' always pairs with 'nor', just as 'either' always pairs with 'or'. These are called correlative conjunctions and must be used as a pair. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The man ___ car was blocking the driveway came to move it.'
questions.explanation: 'Whose' shows possession — the car belonging to the man. 'Who' is for subjects, 'whom' for objects, but only 'whose' works for possession. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We ___ already finished our lunch when the bell rang.'
questions.explanation: 'Had finished' is the past perfect tense, showing that the lunch was completed before the bell rang (another past event). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The homework ___ to be handed in by Friday.'
questions.explanation: 'Homework' is a singular uncountable noun, so it takes the singular verb 'needs'. We say 'the homework needs' not 'the homework need'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'If I ___ you, I would apologise immediately.'
questions.explanation: In imaginary conditional sentences ('if I were you'), we use 'were' for all subjects. This is the subjunctive mood, used for hypothetical situations. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She sings much ___ than her sister.'
questions.explanation: When comparing two people, we use the comparative form. The comparative of 'good' is 'better' (not 'more good' or 'gooder'). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The orchestra, together with the choir, ___ performing at the concert tonight.'
questions.explanation: The subject is 'The orchestra' (singular). 'Together with the choir' is additional information and doesn't make the subject plural. So we use 'is'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'By this time next year, I ___ completed all my exams.'
questions.explanation: 'Will have completed' is the future perfect tense. It describes something that will be finished before a specific point in the future. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The cat sat on ___ mat by the fire.'
questions.explanation: 'The' is used because we're talking about a specific mat (the one by the fire), not just any mat. 'A' or 'an' would be for any mat. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Not only ___ she win the race, but she also broke the school record.'
questions.explanation: 'Not only did she win' uses inverted word order (verb before subject). 'Did' is needed because the sentence is in past tense and uses inversion for emphasis. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The dogs ___ barking at the postman every morning.'
questions.explanation: 'Dogs' is a plural noun, so it needs the plural verb 'are'. Singular nouns use 'is', but plural nouns use 'are'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'My sister ___ to school by bus.'
questions.explanation: 'My sister' is a singular subject (one person), so the verb needs an 's' on the end: 'goes'. We only use 'go' with plural subjects like 'they go' or 'we go'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The box of chocolates ___ on the kitchen table.'
questions.explanation: The subject is 'box' (singular), not 'chocolates'. The phrase 'of chocolates' just describes the box. A singular subject needs a singular verb: 'was'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Each of the children ___ given a certificate at the end of term.'
questions.explanation: 'Each' means every single one, treated individually, so it takes a singular verb: 'was'. Even though there are many children, 'each' focuses on one at a time. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The team ___ celebrating their victory on the pitch.'
questions.explanation: A collective noun like 'team' can take a plural verb when we think of the members acting individually. Here, 'are celebrating' is correct because each member is celebrating. The clue is the word 'their'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Neither the headteacher nor the governors ___ aware of the problem.'
questions.explanation: With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the subject closest to it. 'Governors' is plural, so we use the plural verb 'are'. If the singular noun were closer, we would use a singular verb. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Every pupil and every teacher ___ expected to attend the assembly.'
questions.explanation: When 'every' appears before each noun in a list, it emphasises each individual separately, so the verb is singular: 'is'. This is true even when there are two nouns joined by 'and'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'There ___ several reasons why we should recycle more.'
questions.explanation: The real subject here is 'reasons' (plural), not 'there'. Since 'reasons' is plural, we need the plural verb 'are'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The number of pupils who walk to school ___ increased this year.'
questions.explanation: 'The number of' is a singular phrase (it refers to one number), so it takes the singular verb 'has'. Be careful: 'a number of' would be plural ('a number of pupils have...'), but 'the number of' is always singular. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Mum and Dad ___ coming to watch the school play.'
questions.explanation: 'Mum and Dad' is a compound subject (two people), so it is plural and needs the plural verb 'are'. When two subjects are joined by 'and', they usually take a plural verb. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Yesterday, we ___ to the seaside for a picnic.'
questions.explanation: 'Yesterday' tells us this happened in the past, so we need the past tense: 'went'. 'Go' is present tense and 'gone' is a past participle that needs a helper verb (e.g. 'have gone'). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I ___ my homework before tea every day.'
questions.explanation: 'Every day' tells us this is a regular habit in the present. With 'I', we use 'do' (not 'does', which goes with he/she/it). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She has ___ her best in every race this season.'
questions.explanation: After 'has' we need the past participle: 'done'. 'Has did' and 'has do' are incorrect. The present perfect tense is formed with 'has/have + past participle'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'By the time we arrived, the film had already ___.'
questions.explanation: This is the past perfect tense: 'had started'. It shows that one past event (the film starting) happened before another past event (arriving). The past perfect uses 'had + past participle'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Tomorrow, we ___ visit Grandma in hospital.'
questions.explanation: 'Tomorrow' tells us this is in the future. 'Will' is used to form the future tense: 'will visit'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I have ___ that film three times already.'
questions.explanation: After 'have' we need the past participle: 'seen'. 'Saw' is the simple past (I saw), but with 'have' we must use 'seen' (I have seen). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He ___ quietly in the library when the fire alarm went off.'
questions.explanation: The past continuous tense ('was reading') describes an ongoing action that was interrupted by another event ('the fire alarm went off'). It sets the scene for what happened next. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Had she ___ earlier, she would have caught the train.'
questions.explanation: 'Had she left earlier' is the past perfect in an inverted conditional (instead of 'If she had left earlier'). This formal structure drops 'if' and inverts the subject and auxiliary: 'Had she left...' The past participle 'left' is needed after 'had'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She has ___ to France twice this year.'
questions.explanation: After 'has' we need the past participle. The past participle of 'go' when talking about visiting places is 'been': 'has been'. 'Went' is the simple past and cannot follow 'has'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The children ___ playing in the garden right now.'
questions.explanation: 'Right now' tells us this is happening at the present moment. 'Children' is plural, so we need 'are': 'are playing'. This is the present continuous tense. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I wish I ___ brought my umbrella; now I am soaking wet.'
questions.explanation: 'I wish I had brought...' expresses regret about the past. After 'wish' referring to past events, we use the past perfect ('had + past participle'). This shows something we wanted to happen but did not. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'By next Friday, we ___ finished all our exams.'
questions.explanation: This describes something that will be completed before a future time ('by next Friday'). The future perfect tense 'will have finished' is used when an action will be complete before a specific point in the future. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Mum gave the presents to my brother and ___.'
questions.explanation: After a preposition like 'to', we use the object pronoun 'me' (not 'I'). A good test: remove 'my brother and' — you would say 'Mum gave the presents to me', not 'to I'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Tom and ___ went to the cinema after school.'
questions.explanation: 'I' is correct because it is the subject of the verb 'went'. A good test: remove 'Tom and' — you would say 'I went to the cinema', not 'me went to the cinema'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The teacher asked Oliver and ___ to tidy the classroom.'
questions.explanation: 'Me' is correct because it is the object of the verb 'asked'. Remove 'Oliver and' to test: 'The teacher asked me to tidy' sounds right; 'The teacher asked I to tidy' does not. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ do you think will win the spelling competition?'
questions.explanation: 'Who' is the subject form, used when asking about the person doing the action. Here, someone 'will win', so 'who' is correct. 'Whom' is used for objects (e.g. 'To whom did you give it?'). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'To ___ should I address this letter?'
questions.explanation: 'Whom' is the object form of 'who', used after prepositions. After 'to', we need 'whom': 'To whom should I address this letter?' A useful test: if you can answer with 'him' or 'her', use 'whom'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The cat licked ___ paw after stepping in a puddle.'
questions.explanation: 'Its' (without an apostrophe) is the possessive form meaning 'belonging to it'. 'It's' (with an apostrophe) means 'it is' or 'it has', which does not make sense here. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Everyone must bring ___ own packed lunch on the trip.'
questions.explanation: 'Everyone' refers to all people in a group, and in modern English we use 'their' as a gender-neutral singular pronoun to go with it: 'Everyone must bring their own packed lunch.' ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Between you and ___, I think the test was quite easy.'
questions.explanation: After the preposition 'between', we need the object pronoun 'me'. 'Between you and I' is a very common mistake. The rule is: prepositions (between, for, to, with) are always followed by object pronouns (me, him, her, them). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I wanted to play outside, ___ it was raining heavily.'
questions.explanation: 'But' is used to show a contrast between two ideas. The speaker wanted to play outside, however the rain stopped them. 'But' connects these two opposing ideas. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Would you like juice ___ water with your lunch?'
questions.explanation: 'Or' is used when offering a choice between two options. The question asks you to pick one: juice or water. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ it was cold outside, the children still played in the park.'
questions.explanation: 'Although' introduces a contrast: the cold weather did not stop the children from playing. It means 'even though' or 'despite the fact that'. 'Because' would mean the cold was the reason they played, which does not make sense. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The cake that Mum ___ tasted absolutely delicious.'
questions.explanation: 'Baked' is the correct past tense form. The sentence describes a completed action — Mum baked the cake and then it was tasted. The relative clause 'that Mum baked' describes which cake. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We missed the train; ___, we had to wait an hour for the next one.'
questions.explanation: 'Therefore' shows a result or consequence. Missing the train caused them to wait. 'Therefore' means 'for that reason' or 'as a result'. Using a semicolon before a conjunctive adverb like 'therefore' is a more advanced punctuation pattern. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The match was cancelled ___ the pitch was waterlogged after three days of rain.'
questions.explanation: 'Because' gives the reason for something. The waterlogged pitch was the reason the match was cancelled. 'Although' and 'despite' would suggest the match went ahead anyway, which contradicts the sentence. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'You may go outside to play ___ you finish your homework first.'
questions.explanation: 'Provided' (or 'provided that') sets a condition: you can go outside only if you finish your homework. It means 'on the condition that' or 'as long as'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The museum was fascinating; ___, the gift shop was disappointing.'
questions.explanation: 'Nevertheless' means 'in spite of that' or 'even so'. It connects two contrasting ideas: the museum was great, but the gift shop was not. It is a more formal alternative to 'however'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The cat jumped ___ the wall and into the garden.'
questions.explanation: 'Over' means moving across the top of something. The cat jumped over the wall (from one side to the other). 'Above' means higher than but does not suggest movement across. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'This recipe is very different ___ the one Grandma makes.'
questions.explanation: In British English, 'different from' is the correct form. 'Different to' is sometimes used informally, and 'different than' is American English. In formal writing and exams, always use 'different from'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The prize was shared ___ the two winners.'
questions.explanation: 'Between' is used when talking about two people or things. 'Among' is used for three or more. Since there are exactly two winners here, 'between' is correct. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The sweets were shared ___ all the children in the class.'
questions.explanation: 'Among' is used when something is shared or divided between three or more people. A whole class has many children, so 'among' is the correct choice. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We have PE lessons ___ Tuesdays and Thursdays.'
questions.explanation: We use 'on' with specific days of the week: on Monday, on Tuesday, on Wednesdays. We use 'at' for times (at 3 o'clock) and 'in' for months, seasons, and years (in June, in winter). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The teacher insisted ___ everyone completing the test in silence.'
questions.explanation: 'Insist on' is the correct prepositional pairing. Some verbs always go with specific prepositions: insist on, depend on, listen to, look at. These are called dependent prepositions and must be learnt. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'You ___ brush your teeth before going to bed.'
questions.explanation: 'Should' is used for advice or things that are the right thing to do. Brushing teeth before bed is something you ought to do, making 'should' the best choice. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'It ___ rain this afternoon, so take an umbrella just in case.'
questions.explanation: 'Might' expresses possibility — something that could happen but is not certain. The phrase 'just in case' confirms that the rain is not definite. 'Will' would mean it is certain; 'must' would be too strong. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ I borrow your rubber, please?'
questions.explanation: 'May' is used for polite requests and asking permission. 'May I borrow...?' is more formal and polite than 'Can I borrow...?' In an exam, 'may' is the best choice for asking permission. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'You ___ not run in the corridors; it is a school rule.'
questions.explanation: 'Must not' (mustn't) is used for strict rules and prohibitions. Since this is described as 'a school rule', the strongest modal verb is needed. 'Should not' would be advice, but 'must not' is a firm instruction. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'If I had studied harder, I ___ have passed the test.'
questions.explanation: 'Could have passed' expresses a past possibility that did not happen. This is called the third conditional: 'If I had done X, I could/would have done Y.' It describes an imaginary outcome of an imaginary past action. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The cake ___ baked by Grandma for the school fair.'
questions.explanation: This is a passive voice sentence. The cake did not do the baking — Grandma did. In the passive voice, we use 'was + past participle': 'The cake was baked by Grandma.' ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The windows ___ cleaned every Friday by the caretaker.'
questions.explanation: 'Windows' is plural and 'every Friday' tells us this is a regular present action. In the passive voice with a plural subject, we use 'are + past participle': 'The windows are cleaned.' ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The letter ___ been delivered to the wrong address by mistake.'
questions.explanation: 'Has been delivered' is the present perfect passive. 'Letter' is singular, so it takes 'has' (not 'have'). The full passive structure is: subject + has/have + been + past participle. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The new playground ___ being built behind the school hall.'
questions.explanation: 'Is being built' is the present continuous passive. It describes something happening right now. 'Playground' is singular, so we use 'is' (not 'are'). The structure is: subject + is/are + being + past participle. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'All the biscuits have been ___ by the time I got home.'
questions.explanation: In the passive voice, we need the past participle after 'have been': 'have been eaten'. The past participle of 'eat' is 'eaten' (not 'ate', which is the simple past). Recognising the passive perfect construction ('have been + past participle') is an advanced grammar skill. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The girl ___ sits next to me is called Sophie.'
questions.explanation: 'Who' is used for people. The girl is a person, and she is the subject of the relative clause (she sits next to me), so 'who' is correct. 'Which' is used for things, not people. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The book ___ I borrowed from the library was really exciting.'
questions.explanation: 'Which' is used for things (not people). The book is a thing, so we use 'which'. 'That' could also work here, but 'who' is only used for people. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The boy ___ bag was stolen reported it to the office.'
questions.explanation: 'Whose' shows possession — it means 'belonging to whom'. The bag belonged to the boy, so 'whose' is the correct relative pronoun: 'the boy whose bag was stolen'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The park ___ we play football is near the river.'
questions.explanation: 'Where' is used for places. The park is a place, so 'where we play football' is correct. It means 'in which we play football'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The author ___ novel won the prize gave a talk at our school.'
questions.explanation: 'Whose' shows possession and works for both people and things. The novel belonged to the author, so we say 'the author whose novel won the prize'. Do not confuse 'whose' with 'who's' (which means 'who is'). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'My sister is ___ than me at swimming.'
questions.explanation: 'Better' is the comparative form of 'good'. We never say 'more good' — it is an irregular adjective: good, better, best. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'That was the ___ film I have ever seen.'
questions.explanation: 'Worst' is the superlative form of 'bad'. When comparing more than two things (every film ever seen), we use the superlative. 'Bad' is irregular: bad, worse, worst. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'This maths problem is ___ difficult than the last one.'
questions.explanation: For longer adjectives (two or more syllables), we use 'more' to form the comparative: 'more difficult'. We do not say 'difficulter'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Of all the pupils in the class, Amira runs the ___.'
questions.explanation: 'Fastest' is the superlative form of 'fast'. Short adjectives and adverbs add '-est' for the superlative. 'Of all the pupils' tells us we are comparing the whole class, so the superlative is needed. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The weather today is ___ than it was yesterday, but not as bad as Monday.'
questions.explanation: 'Worse' is the comparative form of 'bad'. We are comparing two things (today vs yesterday), so the comparative is needed. 'More worse' is always wrong — never use 'more' with an irregular comparative. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ going to be late if we do not hurry.'
questions.explanation: 'They're' is short for 'they are'. The sentence means 'They are going to be late', so 'they're' is correct. 'Their' means belonging to them, and 'there' refers to a place. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The children left ___ coats in the cloakroom.'
questions.explanation: 'Their' shows possession — the coats belong to the children. 'There' refers to a place and 'they're' means 'they are', neither of which fits here. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ not allowed to use your phone during lessons.'
questions.explanation: 'You're' is short for 'you are'. The sentence means 'You are not allowed', so 'you're' is correct. 'Your' shows possession (your phone, your book) and is used later in the same sentence. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I find it hard to tell whether ___ going to rain or not.'
questions.explanation: 'It's' is short for 'it is'. The sentence means '...whether it is going to rain'. 'Its' (without an apostrophe) shows possession (e.g. 'the dog wagged its tail'). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'There is ___ much sugar in this recipe; I only need a little.'
questions.explanation: 'Too' means 'more than needed' or 'also'. Here it means there is more sugar than necessary. 'To' is a preposition or part of an infinitive verb, and 'two' is the number 2. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We walked ___ the park to get to the swimming pool on the other side.'
questions.explanation: 'Through' means moving in one side and out the other. 'Threw' is the past tense of 'throw' and sounds the same but has a completely different meaning. 'Though' means 'although' and 'thorough' means careful and detailed. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The cat ___ sleeping on the sofa.'
questions.explanation: 'Cat' is a singular noun, so it needs the singular verb 'is'. Plural nouns like 'cats' would take 'are'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'My friends ___ coming to my birthday party on Saturday.'
questions.explanation: 'Friends' is a plural noun (more than one friend), so it takes the plural verb 'are'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'There ___ a spider in the bath.'
questions.explanation: The real subject is 'a spider' (singular), so we use 'is'. If there were multiple spiders, we would say 'there are'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The bag of sweets ___ on the top shelf.'
questions.explanation: The subject is 'bag' (singular), not 'sweets'. The phrase 'of sweets' just describes the bag. A singular subject takes a singular verb: 'is'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'One of the windows ___ broken during the storm.'
questions.explanation: The subject is 'one' (singular), not 'windows'. 'One of the windows' refers to a single window, so we use the singular verb 'was'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The class ___ going on a trip to the museum next week.'
questions.explanation: 'Class' is a collective noun. When the group acts together as one unit (going on a trip), we can use the singular verb 'is'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Nobody in my family ___ how to speak French.'
questions.explanation: 'Nobody' is always singular, so it takes the singular verb 'knows' with an 's'. Even though 'family' is mentioned, 'nobody' is the subject. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Either the twins or their older sister ___ responsible for walking the dog.'
questions.explanation: With 'either...or', the verb agrees with the subject closest to it. 'Their older sister' is singular and closest to the verb, so we use 'is'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'A number of pupils ___ absent today because of the flu.'
questions.explanation: 'A number of' means 'several' and takes a plural verb: 'a number of pupils are'. This is different from 'the number of', which is singular. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Five pounds ___ not enough to buy that book.'
questions.explanation: When we talk about an amount of money as a single sum, it takes a singular verb. 'Five pounds' is treated as one amount, so we say 'five pounds is'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The birds in the garden ___ singing loudly.'
questions.explanation: 'Birds' is plural, so it needs the plural verb 'are'. The phrase 'in the garden' just tells us where the birds are. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Fish and chips ___ my favourite dinner.'
questions.explanation: 'Fish and chips' is treated as one dish (a single meal), not two separate foods. When two nouns joined by 'and' form a single idea, they take a singular verb: 'is'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Mathematics ___ my favourite subject at school.'
questions.explanation: 'Mathematics' looks plural because it ends in 's', but it is actually a singular noun (one subject). Other examples: physics is, gymnastics is, news is. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ a new bicycle for her birthday.'
questions.explanation: 'She' is a singular subject (third person), so the verb needs an 's': 'wants'. We use 'want' without the 's' for I, you, we, and they. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Everybody ___ excited about the school trip to London.'
questions.explanation: 'Everybody' is always treated as singular, even though it refers to many people. So we use 'is', not 'are'. The same applies to 'everyone', 'someone', and 'anyone'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The sheep ___ grazing in the field near the farmhouse.'
questions.explanation: 'Sheep' can be singular or plural. The context here (a field of sheep grazing) suggests more than one, so 'are' is used. If it were one sheep, 'is' would be correct. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'My teacher ___ very kind and always helps us.'
questions.explanation: 'My teacher' is one person (singular), so it takes the singular verb 'is'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Neither of the answers ___ correct.'
questions.explanation: 'Neither' means 'not one and not the other'. It is always singular, so we use 'is'. Even though there are two answers, 'neither' refers to each one individually. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The jury ___ unable to agree on a verdict.'
questions.explanation: When a collective noun like 'jury' refers to the members acting individually (they cannot agree), we use a plural verb: 'are'. The inability to agree shows they are acting as separate people, not as one unit. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Last summer, we ___ to Cornwall for our holiday.'
questions.explanation: 'Last summer' tells us this happened in the past. The past tense of 'go' is 'went'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ her breakfast every morning before school.'
questions.explanation: 'Every morning' tells us this is a regular habit in the present. With 'she' (third person singular), we add 's' to the verb: 'eats'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Right now, the children ___ building a sandcastle on the beach.'
questions.explanation: 'Right now' tells us this is happening at the present moment. 'Children' is plural, so we need 'are': 'are building'. This is the present continuous tense. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I ___ a lovely card from Grandma last week.'
questions.explanation: 'Last week' tells us this happened in the past, so we need the past simple tense: 'received'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'They have ___ to that restaurant many times before.'
questions.explanation: After 'have', we need the past participle. When talking about visiting a place, we use 'been': 'have been to'. 'Went' is the simple past and cannot follow 'have'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We were ___ our dinner when the doorbell rang.'
questions.explanation: 'Were eating' is the past continuous tense. It describes an ongoing action in the past that was interrupted by another event (the doorbell ringing). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She has ___ the piano since she was five years old.'
questions.explanation: After 'has', we need the past participle: 'has played'. This is the present perfect tense, used for actions that started in the past and continue to the present. 'Since she was five' tells us it is still ongoing. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'By the time Mum arrived, the children had already ___ to sleep.'
questions.explanation: 'Had gone' is the past perfect tense. It shows that one past event (going to sleep) happened before another past event (Mum arriving). After 'had', we need the past participle: 'gone'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Next week, our class ___ visit the science museum.'
questions.explanation: 'Next week' tells us this is in the future. 'Will' forms the simple future tense: 'will visit'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I have never ___ a whale before.'
questions.explanation: After 'have', we need the past participle: 'seen'. This is the present perfect tense, used for experiences up to now. 'Saw' is the simple past and cannot follow 'have'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'While Dad ___ washing the car, it started to rain.'
questions.explanation: 'Was washing' is the past continuous tense. It describes an ongoing action in the past that was interrupted by another event (the rain starting). 'Dad' is singular, so we use 'was'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ in London for three years before moving to Bristol.'
questions.explanation: 'Had lived' is the past perfect tense. It shows that her time in London was completed before another past event (moving to Bristol). The past perfect is used when two events happened in the past and we need to show which came first. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'By next summer, I ___ finished primary school.'
questions.explanation: 'Will have finished' is the future perfect tense. It describes something that will be completed before a specific future time ('by next summer'). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He ___ a picture of his dog yesterday.'
questions.explanation: 'Yesterday' tells us this happened in the past. The past tense of 'draw' is 'drew'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The baby ___ crying because she is hungry.'
questions.explanation: 'Baby' is singular, so we use 'is'. 'Is crying' is the present continuous tense, describing what is happening right now. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We ___ just finished lunch when the phone rang.'
questions.explanation: 'Had just finished' is the past perfect tense. It shows that finishing lunch happened just before another past event (the phone ringing). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I wish I ___ more time to read.'
questions.explanation: After 'wish', we use the past tense to talk about something we want but do not have right now. 'I wish I had more time' expresses a desire for something that is not true in the present. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'If she ___ earlier, she would not have missed the bus.'
questions.explanation: This is the third conditional, used for imaginary past situations. The 'if' clause uses the past perfect: 'if she had left'. The result clause uses 'would have + past participle'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Tom ___ his teeth every night before bed.'
questions.explanation: 'Every night' tells us this is a regular habit. With 'Tom' (he, third person singular), we add 'es' to the verb: 'brushes'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ reading her book when the lights went out.'
questions.explanation: 'Was reading' is the past continuous tense. It describes an action that was happening when it was interrupted by another event ('the lights went out'). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'They had ___ all the cakes before the guests arrived.'
questions.explanation: After 'had', we need the past participle: 'eaten'. This is the past perfect tense, showing that one action (eating the cakes) was completed before another past action (the guests arriving). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I ___ my Gran every Sunday afternoon.'
questions.explanation: 'Every Sunday' tells us this is a regular habit in the present. With 'I', we use the base form: 'visit' (not 'visits', which is for he/she/it). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Have you ever ___ on an aeroplane?'
questions.explanation: After 'have', we need the past participle: 'flown'. 'Fly' is an irregular verb: fly, flew, flown. The present perfect 'have you ever flown' asks about a life experience. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She told me she ___ already posted the letter.'
questions.explanation: When reporting what someone said in the past, we use the past perfect. 'She told me she had posted' — 'had' is needed because we shift one tense further back when using reported speech. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Look! It ___ snowing outside!'
questions.explanation: 'Look!' tells us this is happening right now. 'It is snowing' uses the present continuous tense for an action in progress at this moment. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'By the end of this term, we ___ learnt all our times tables.'
questions.explanation: 'By the end of this term' refers to a future point in time. The future perfect 'will have learnt' describes something that will be completed before that future point. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ practising the violin for two hours when her teacher arrived.'
questions.explanation: 'Had been practising' is the past perfect continuous tense. It describes an ongoing action in the past that continued up to another past event (the teacher arriving). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The dog ___ a hole in the garden last night.'
questions.explanation: 'Last night' tells us this happened in the past. The past tense of 'dig' is 'dug'. It is an irregular verb. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I ___ never tasted mango before I went to India.'
questions.explanation: 'Had never tasted' is the past perfect tense. It describes an experience (or lack of one) that was true up until a past event (going to India). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'My brother is ___ than me.'
questions.explanation: When comparing two people, we use the comparative form. For short adjectives like 'tall', we add '-er': 'taller'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Mount Everest is the ___ mountain in the world.'
questions.explanation: 'The highest' is the superlative form of 'high'. When comparing something to all others, we use the superlative. For short adjectives, add '-est'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'This book is ___ than the one I read last week.'
questions.explanation: For longer adjectives (three or more syllables like 'interesting'), we use 'more' for the comparative: 'more interesting'. We never add '-er' to long adjectives. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Of all the subjects, I think science is the ___.'
questions.explanation: 'Of all the subjects' means we are comparing more than two things, so we need the superlative. For long adjectives, we use 'most': 'the most exciting'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Today's test was ___ than yesterday's.'
questions.explanation: For two-syllable adjectives ending in 'y', we change the 'y' to 'i' and add '-er': 'easier'. We do not say 'more easy'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He is the ___ runner on the whole athletics team.'
questions.explanation: 'The slowest' is the superlative of 'slow'. When comparing one person to everyone else in a group, we use the superlative. For short adjectives, add '-est'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The further we walked, the ___ the path became.'
questions.explanation: 'The further...the narrower' is a comparative structure showing two things changing together. For two-syllable adjectives like 'narrow', we can add '-er': 'narrower'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She speaks ___ than her little sister.'
questions.explanation: When comparing two people, we use the comparative. For short adjectives like 'loud', add '-er': 'louder'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'That was the ___ story I have ever heard.'
questions.explanation: 'I have ever heard' compares to all stories, so we need the superlative. For adjectives ending in 'y', change 'y' to 'i' and add '-est': 'funniest'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'This homework is ___ difficult than last week's, but it is not the most difficult we have had.'
questions.explanation: 'Less' is the comparative form of 'little' when used with uncountable or abstract qualities. 'Less difficult' means not as difficult. 'Fewer' is for countable nouns. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'My bag is ___ than yours.'
questions.explanation: When comparing two things, we use the comparative. For adjectives ending in 'y', change 'y' to 'i' and add '-er': 'heavier'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She is the ___ person I know.'
questions.explanation: 'Generous' is a long adjective (three syllables), so we use 'most' for the superlative: 'the most generous'. We never add '-est' to long adjectives. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Of the two twins, Jack is the ___.'
questions.explanation: When comparing exactly two people, we use the comparative form: 'the taller'. The superlative ('tallest') is only used when comparing three or more. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'This cake tastes ___ than the one we had last time.'
questions.explanation: The comparative of 'good' is 'better' (not 'gooder' or 'more good'). It is an irregular adjective: good, better, best. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She felt ___ after a good night's sleep.'
questions.explanation: 'Better' is the comparative of 'good' (or 'well' when talking about health). After sleeping well, she felt improved — 'better'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The situation grew ___ and worse as the day went on.'
questions.explanation: 'Worse and worse' describes something getting increasingly bad over time. 'Worse' is the comparative form of 'bad'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'An elephant is much ___ than a mouse.'
questions.explanation: When comparing two things, we use the comparative. For short adjectives ending in a consonant, we double the final letter and add '-er': 'bigger'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She drives more ___ than her husband.'
questions.explanation: We need an adverb to describe how she drives. The comparative adverb form is 'more carefully'. With long adverbs, we use 'more' rather than adding '-er'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'That is by far the ___ beautiful painting in the gallery.'
questions.explanation: 'By far' emphasises the superlative. 'The most beautiful' is the superlative form of 'beautiful' (a long adjective). 'By far the most' means it is clearly the best. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'There were ___ mistakes in Tom's work than in mine.'
questions.explanation: 'Fewer' is used for countable nouns like 'mistakes'. 'Less' is for uncountable nouns like 'water' or 'time'. Since you can count mistakes, 'fewer' is correct. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Either you tidy your room ___ you will not be allowed to watch television.'
questions.explanation: 'Either' always pairs with 'or': either...or. This gives a choice between two options: tidy your room, or no television. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Neither the cake ___ the biscuits were left after the party.'
questions.explanation: 'Neither' always pairs with 'nor': neither...nor. This means not the cake and not the biscuits — both were eaten. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Not only did she finish first, ___ she also broke the record.'
questions.explanation: 'Not only...but also' is a correlative conjunction pair. It emphasises two impressive things: finishing first AND breaking the record. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Both the headteacher ___ the deputy were present at the meeting.'
questions.explanation: 'Both' always pairs with 'and': both...and. This means the headteacher was there AND the deputy was there. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Whether we go to the beach ___ to the park, I will be happy.'
questions.explanation: 'Whether' pairs with 'or': whether...or. This presents two alternatives — the beach or the park. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Not only ___ he kind, but he is also very generous.'
questions.explanation: 'Not only is he kind' uses inverted word order (verb before subject) for emphasis. The present tense 'is' matches the second part ('he is also very generous'). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She can either walk to school ___ take the bus.'
questions.explanation: 'Either' always pairs with 'or'. This gives a choice between two options: walking or taking the bus. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Neither my brother ___ my sister likes Brussels sprouts.'
questions.explanation: 'Neither' always pairs with 'nor': neither...nor. This means my brother does not like sprouts and my sister does not like sprouts either. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'No sooner had we sat down ___ the fire alarm went off.'
questions.explanation: 'No sooner...than' is a correlative conjunction pair meaning something happened immediately after something else. 'No sooner had we sat down than the alarm went off.' ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I would like both a sandwich ___ a drink, please.'
questions.explanation: 'Both' pairs with 'and': both...and. The speaker wants a sandwich AND a drink — not one or the other. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Either the red shirt ___ the blue one will go with those trousers.'
questions.explanation: 'Either' pairs with 'or'. This means one of the two shirts will work — the red one or the blue one. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Not only ___ the team win the match, but they also remained unbeaten all season.'
questions.explanation: 'Not only did the team win' uses inverted word order with 'did' because the main verb is in the past simple ('win'). The inversion adds emphasis. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Hardly had the match started ___ it began to pour with rain.'
questions.explanation: 'Hardly...when' is a correlative conjunction pair meaning something happened almost immediately after something else. 'Hardly had the match started when it began to rain.' ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'My friend and ___ walked to school together.'
questions.explanation: 'I' is correct because it is the subject of the verb 'walked'. Test by removing 'My friend and': 'I walked to school together' sounds right; 'me walked' does not. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The teacher told James and ___ to sit down.'
questions.explanation: 'Me' is correct because it is the object of the verb 'told'. Test by removing 'James and': 'The teacher told me to sit down' sounds right; 'told I' does not. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The dog that lives next door barks whenever ___ sees a squirrel.'
questions.explanation: We use 'it' as the pronoun for animals (unless we know the animal personally). 'It sees' is correct — 'it' replaces 'the dog'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Someone has left ___ umbrella in the cloakroom.'
questions.explanation: 'Someone' refers to an unknown person, so we use 'their' as a gender-neutral singular pronoun. 'Someone has left their umbrella' is correct in modern English. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Is this book yours or ___?'
questions.explanation: 'Mine' is the possessive pronoun meaning 'belonging to me'. We say 'yours or mine' — we do not say 'yours or my' (which would need a noun after it). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ should I speak to about the lost property?'
questions.explanation: 'Whom' is the object form of 'who', used after prepositions. The full form is 'To whom should I speak?' A useful test: if you can answer with 'him' or 'her', use 'whom'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The cake was made by Lily and ___.'
questions.explanation: 'Me' is correct because it follows the preposition 'by'. After prepositions (by, for, to, with), we use object pronouns: me, him, her, us, them. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ do you think scored the winning goal?'
questions.explanation: 'Who' is the subject form, used when asking about the person doing the action. Someone 'scored' the goal (they did the action), so 'who' is correct. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We painted the fence by ___.'
questions.explanation: 'Ourselves' is a reflexive pronoun meaning 'by ourselves' — without any help from anyone else. When the subject and object are the same, we use a reflexive pronoun. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The twins looked at each other and smiled. Then ___ both started laughing.'
questions.explanation: 'They' is the subject pronoun, replacing 'the twins'. Since the twins are doing the action (laughing), we need the subject form. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'It was ___ who broke the vase, not my brother.'
questions.explanation: After the verb 'to be' (was), the traditional grammar rule uses the subject pronoun: 'It was I who broke the vase.' This is formal English used in 11+ exams. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Mum drove ___ to the swimming pool.'
questions.explanation: 'Us' is the object pronoun — it receives the action. Mum (subject) drove us (object). 'We' is only used as a subject. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'That bicycle over there is ___.'
questions.explanation: 'Hers' is the possessive pronoun meaning 'belonging to her'. We say 'the bicycle is hers'. 'Her' would need a noun after it: 'her bicycle'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The student ___ essay won the prize was very pleased.'
questions.explanation: 'Whose' shows possession — the essay belonging to the student. 'Whose essay won the prize' is a relative clause telling us which student. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She hurt ___ when she fell off the climbing frame.'
questions.explanation: 'Herself' is a reflexive pronoun. When the person doing the action and the person affected are the same (she hurt herself), we use a reflexive pronoun. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Dad asked my sister and ___ to help with the washing up.'
questions.explanation: 'Me' is correct because it is the object of 'asked'. Test by removing 'my sister and': 'Dad asked me to help' sounds right; 'Dad asked I' does not. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'These seats are for you and ___.'
questions.explanation: After the preposition 'for', we use the object pronoun 'me'. 'For you and me' is correct. 'For you and I' is a very common mistake. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ of the children brought their own packed lunch.'
questions.explanation: 'Each' emphasises every individual one in the group. 'Each of the children' focuses on them one by one. 'All' could also work but with different emphasis. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Give the pencil to ___.'
questions.explanation: 'Him' is the object pronoun. After prepositions like 'to', we use object pronouns: me, him, her, us, them. 'Give the pencil to him' is correct. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'My birthday is ___ March.'
questions.explanation: We use 'in' with months: in March, in June, in December. We use 'on' with specific dates (on 5th March) and 'at' with times (at 3 o'clock). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'School starts ___ nine o'clock.'
questions.explanation: We use 'at' with specific times: at nine o'clock, at half past three, at noon. We use 'on' for days and 'in' for months and years. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The picture is hanging ___ the wall.'
questions.explanation: We use 'on' for surfaces. A picture hangs on a wall, a book sits on a table, a sticker goes on paper. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He is very good ___ playing the piano.'
questions.explanation: 'Good at' is the correct combination. We say 'good at something' or 'good at doing something'. This is a dependent preposition that must be learnt. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The train arrived ___ the platform five minutes early.'
questions.explanation: We say 'at' for a specific point or location. The train arrives 'at' the platform, 'at' the station, 'at' the airport. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She walked ___ the bridge to get to the other side of the river.'
questions.explanation: 'Across' means from one side to the other of a surface or flat area. Walking across a bridge takes you from one side to the other. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The rabbit ran ___ the hole in the fence.'
questions.explanation: 'Through' means going in one side and out the other. The rabbit went through the hole — entering one side of the fence and coming out the other. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The children were sitting ___ the teacher in a semicircle.'
questions.explanation: 'Around' means positioned in a circle or curve surrounding something. The children sat in a semicircle around the teacher. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I am looking forward ___ the school trip next week.'
questions.explanation: 'Look forward to' is a phrasal verb meaning to feel excited about something in the future. The preposition is always 'to'. Note: if a verb follows, it must be in the '-ing' form: 'looking forward to going'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The ball rolled ___ the table and fell on the floor.'
questions.explanation: 'Off' means away from a surface. The ball was on the table, then it rolled off (away from) the table. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She is allergic ___ nuts.'
questions.explanation: 'Allergic to' is the correct combination. We always say 'allergic to something', never 'allergic of' or 'allergic from'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The teacher reminded us ___ the homework deadline.'
questions.explanation: 'Remind someone about something' is correct. We can also say 'remind someone of something'. Both mean to help someone remember. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Put your coat ___ before you go outside.'
questions.explanation: 'Put on' means to place clothing on your body. It is a phrasal verb: 'put on your coat'. The opposite is 'take off'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The school is famous ___ its excellent choir.'
questions.explanation: 'Famous for' is the correct combination. We say something is 'famous for' a quality or achievement. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She apologised ___ being late to the lesson.'
questions.explanation: 'Apologise for' is the correct combination. We apologise 'for' something we have done wrong. 'Apologise to' is used for the person: 'She apologised to the teacher for being late.' ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The cat is hiding ___ the bed.'
questions.explanation: 'Under' means below or beneath. The cat is hiding underneath the bed, in the space below it. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The homework must be handed ___ by Friday.'
questions.explanation: 'Hand in' means to submit or give something to a teacher or authority. 'Handed in' is the past participle used in the passive voice here. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We need to agree ___ a date for the school fair.'
questions.explanation: 'Agree on' is used when people need to reach a decision together about something specific. We 'agree on' a date, a plan, or a price. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'You ___ eat your vegetables if you want to stay healthy.'
questions.explanation: 'Should' is used for advice or recommendations. Eating vegetables is good advice for staying healthy. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'You ___ not talk during the test; it is strictly forbidden.'
questions.explanation: 'Must not' is used for strict rules and prohibitions. Since talking during a test is 'strictly forbidden', 'must not' is the strongest and most appropriate choice. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We ___ go to the park later if it stops raining.'
questions.explanation: 'Could' expresses a possibility. It is not certain — it depends on the rain stopping. 'Could go' means it is possible but not definite. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ have arrived by now; the train left an hour ago.'
questions.explanation: 'Should have arrived' expresses an expectation about the past. Based on the time the train left, we would expect her to have arrived already. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ you help me carry these books, please?'
questions.explanation: 'Would' is used for polite requests. 'Would you help me?' is a courteous way to ask for assistance. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'You should ___ told me about the change of plan.'
questions.explanation: 'Should have' is correct. A very common mistake is writing 'should of' because it sounds similar when spoken quickly. But 'of' is a preposition, not a verb — only 'have' works here. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'It ___ snow tomorrow according to the weather forecast.'
questions.explanation: 'Might' expresses possibility — something that could happen but is not certain. A weather forecast gives a prediction, not a certainty, so 'might' is the best choice. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'If I had known about the party, I ___ have come.'
questions.explanation: 'Would have come' is the third conditional result clause. It describes an imaginary outcome of an imaginary past condition: 'If I had known (but I did not), I would have come (but I did not).' ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'You ___ ask permission before using someone else's belongings.'
questions.explanation: 'Must' expresses a strong obligation or duty. Asking permission before using others' things is something you are required to do. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ swim when she was only three years old.'
questions.explanation: 'Could' is the past tense of 'can'. Since the sentence talks about the past ('when she was three'), we use 'could' to describe an ability she had then. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'They ___ not have left already; I saw their car in the driveway.'
questions.explanation: 'Can't have' or 'cannot have' expresses that something is logically impossible based on evidence. The car in the driveway proves they have not left. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ I open the window? It is very warm in here.'
questions.explanation: 'Shall I' is used to offer to do something or to make a suggestion. 'Shall I open the window?' politely offers to help. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He could ___ told us earlier instead of waiting until the last minute.'
questions.explanation: 'Could have told' is the correct form expressing a past possibility that did not happen. 'Could of' is always wrong — 'of' is not a verb. The confusion arises because 'could've' sounds like 'could of' when spoken. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We ___ wear school uniform every day.'
questions.explanation: 'Must' expresses a strong obligation. Wearing school uniform is a rule that has to be followed every day. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'You ___ see the doctor if the pain does not go away.'
questions.explanation: 'Ought to' gives advice, similar to 'should'. It suggests the right thing to do. 'You ought to see the doctor' means it would be wise to go. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ have practised more; her performance was not as good as usual.'
questions.explanation: 'Should have practised' expresses regret or criticism about the past. It means she did not practise enough, and the result shows. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I saw ___ elephant at the zoo yesterday.'
questions.explanation: 'An' is used before words that begin with a vowel sound. 'Elephant' starts with an 'e' sound, so we say 'an elephant'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She bought ___ new dress for the party.'
questions.explanation: 'A' is used before words that begin with a consonant sound. 'New' starts with an 'n' sound, so we say 'a new dress'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Please close ___ door behind you.'
questions.explanation: 'The' is used when both the speaker and listener know which specific thing is being talked about. There is one particular door, and both people know which one. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He wants to be ___ honest person.'
questions.explanation: 'An' is used before words that begin with a vowel sound. Although 'honest' starts with the letter 'h', the 'h' is silent, so the word begins with an 'o' sound: 'an honest'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She is studying at ___ university in London.'
questions.explanation: 'A' is used before words that begin with a consonant sound. Although 'university' starts with the letter 'u', it begins with a 'yoo' sound (like 'you'), which is a consonant sound: 'a university'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ sun was shining brightly when we woke up this morning.'
questions.explanation: 'The' is used for unique things — there is only one sun. We always say 'the sun', 'the moon', 'the Earth'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She plays ___ violin beautifully.'
questions.explanation: We use 'the' before musical instruments when talking about playing them: 'play the violin', 'play the piano', 'play the drums'. This is a fixed rule in English. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I would like ___ apple from the fruit bowl, please.'
questions.explanation: 'An' is used before words beginning with a vowel sound. 'Apple' starts with an 'a' sound, so we say 'an apple'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'It was ___ honour to meet the author.'
questions.explanation: 'An' is used because the 'h' in 'honour' is silent, so the word begins with an 'o' vowel sound. We say 'an honour', 'an hour', 'an honest'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'My uncle is ___ MP for our local area.'
questions.explanation: 'An' is used before abbreviations that begin with a vowel sound when spoken. 'MP' is said as 'em-pee', which starts with an 'e' sound, so we say 'an MP'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'There was ___ big spider in the bathroom.'
questions.explanation: 'A' is used before words beginning with a consonant sound. 'Big' starts with a 'b' sound, so we say 'a big spider'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ River Thames flows through London.'
questions.explanation: We use 'the' with named rivers, seas, and oceans: the Thames, the Atlantic, the Pacific. However, we do not use 'the' with most lakes: Lake Windermere (not the Lake Windermere). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He ate ___ whole cake by himself!'
questions.explanation: 'The' is used because we are talking about a specific cake — 'the whole cake'. The speaker and listener both know which cake is being discussed. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She is ___ tallest girl in our class.'
questions.explanation: We always use 'the' with superlatives: 'the tallest', 'the best', 'the most interesting'. The superlative picks out one specific thing from a group. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The book was ___ by a famous author.'
questions.explanation: This is a passive voice sentence. In the passive, we use 'was' + past participle: 'was written'. 'Wrote' is the simple past active form, not the past participle. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The match was ___ because of heavy rain.'
questions.explanation: In the passive voice, we use 'was' + past participle: 'was cancelled'. The match did not cancel itself — it was cancelled by someone else. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The school was ___ in 1920 by a local businessman.'
questions.explanation: 'Was founded' is the correct passive form. 'Found' means 'discovered' (past tense of 'find'), but 'founded' means 'established' or 'set up'. The school was established in 1920. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'All the tickets have been ___.'
questions.explanation: 'Have been sold' is the present perfect passive. After 'have been', we need the past participle: 'sold'. This tells us the selling is now complete. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The walls are being ___ a bright blue colour.'
questions.explanation: 'Are being painted' is the present continuous passive. It describes an action happening right now. The structure is: subject + is/are being + past participle. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The trophy ___ presented by the mayor at the ceremony last night.'
questions.explanation: 'Was presented' is the past simple passive. 'Last night' tells us this is in the past. 'Trophy' is singular, so we use 'was' (not 'were'). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The windows are ___ every week by the cleaner.'
questions.explanation: In the passive voice, we use 'are' + past participle: 'are cleaned'. 'Every week' tells us this is a regular action. 'Windows' is plural, so we use 'are'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The homework had been ___ before the teacher collected it.'
questions.explanation: 'Had been completed' is the past perfect passive. It shows that the homework was finished before another past event (the teacher collecting it). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'English ___ spoken in many countries around the world.'
questions.explanation: 'English' is a singular noun, so it takes 'is'. 'English is spoken' is the present simple passive, describing a general fact that is always true. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The cake was ___ by my mum for the school fair.'
questions.explanation: In the passive voice, we use 'was' + past participle: 'was baked'. The cake did not bake itself — it was baked by Mum. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The new sports hall will be ___ next year.'
questions.explanation: 'Will be opened' is the future passive. After 'will be', we need the past participle: 'opened'. This describes something that will happen in the future. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The suspect is believed to ___ left the country.'
questions.explanation: 'Is believed to have left' is a passive reporting structure. After 'to', we use the infinitive 'have' (not 'has' or 'had'). This means people believe the suspect left the country. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The trees ___ been planted by volunteers last spring.'
questions.explanation: 'Had been planted' is the past perfect passive. 'Last spring' tells us this was in the past, and the action was completed before another reference point. 'Trees' is plural, so 'had' (which works with all subjects) is correct. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The results will ___ announced next Monday.'
questions.explanation: After 'will', we use the base form: 'will be announced'. The future passive structure is: subject + will be + past participle. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The lady ___ lives next door has a lovely garden.'
questions.explanation: 'Who' is used for people. The lady is a person and she is the subject of the relative clause (she lives next door), so 'who' is correct. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The toy ___ I wanted was sold out.'
questions.explanation: 'That' is used for things and can replace 'which' in defining relative clauses. 'The toy that I wanted' tells us which specific toy. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The girl ___ father is a doctor wants to be a nurse.'
questions.explanation: 'Whose' shows possession — the father belongs to the girl. 'The girl whose father is a doctor' tells us which girl we are talking about. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The cinema ___ we saw the film has closed down.'
questions.explanation: 'Where' is used for places. The cinema is a place, so 'the cinema where we saw the film' is correct. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The car ___ was parked outside has been moved.'
questions.explanation: 'Which' is used for things (not people). The car is a thing, so 'which' is the correct relative pronoun. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The teacher ___ I like the most is Mrs Chapman.'
