questions.explanation: 'Who' is used for people in relative clauses. 'The teacher who I like the most' tells us which teacher. In informal English, 'who' can be used as both subject and object. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The day ___ we arrived was cold and rainy.'
questions.explanation: 'When' is used for times. 'The day when we arrived' tells us which day. 'When' replaces 'on which' in relative clauses about time. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The woman to ___ I spoke was very helpful.'
questions.explanation: 'Whom' is the object form of 'who', used after prepositions. 'To whom I spoke' is the formal, grammatically correct form. After prepositions like 'to', 'for', 'with', we use 'whom'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The dog ___ chased the cat belongs to our neighbour.'
questions.explanation: 'That' can be used for both people and things in defining relative clauses. 'The dog that chased the cat' tells us which specific dog. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The park, ___ is near our house, has a wonderful playground.'
questions.explanation: In non-defining relative clauses (with commas), we use 'which' for things, not 'that'. The commas show this is extra information about the park. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The reason ___ she was late was that the bus broke down.'
questions.explanation: 'Why' is used in relative clauses after 'reason'. 'The reason why she was late' explains the cause. Some people also say 'the reason that', but 'why' is more precise. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The boy ___ won the race was only nine years old.'
questions.explanation: 'Who' is used for people. The boy is a person and he is the subject of the relative clause (he won the race), so 'who' is correct. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The house ___ roof was damaged in the storm has been repaired.'
questions.explanation: 'Whose' can be used for both people and things to show possession. The roof belongs to the house: 'the house whose roof was damaged'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'My grandmother, ___ is eighty years old, still plays tennis every week.'
questions.explanation: In non-defining relative clauses (with commas) for people, we use 'who', not 'that'. The clause adds extra information about the grandmother. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Is this the shop ___ you bought your school shoes?'
questions.explanation: 'Where' is used for places. The shop is a place, so 'the shop where you bought your shoes' is correct. It means 'the shop in which'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The children, all of ___ had passed the test, celebrated together.'
questions.explanation: After prepositions like 'of', we use 'whom' for people: 'all of whom'. In non-defining clauses (with commas), 'that' cannot be used. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Put your books over ___.'
questions.explanation: 'There' refers to a place or position. 'Put your books over there' means in that location. 'Their' means belonging to them, and 'they're' means 'they are'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ all going to the park after school.'
questions.explanation: 'They're' is short for 'they are'. The sentence means 'They are all going to the park.' ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The dog chased ___ tail around in circles.'
questions.explanation: 'Its' (without an apostrophe) shows possession — the tail belonging to the dog. 'It's' with an apostrophe means 'it is' or 'it has', which does not fit here. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ a lovely day for a walk along the beach.'
questions.explanation: 'It's' is short for 'it is'. The sentence means 'It is a lovely day for a walk.' ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Is that ___ pencil case or mine?'
questions.explanation: 'Your' shows possession — the pencil case belonging to you. 'Your pencil case' needs the possessive determiner. 'You're' means 'you are'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ going to be the best school play ever!'
questions.explanation: 'It's' is short for 'it is'. The sentence means 'It is going to be the best school play ever!' ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The children lost ___ way in the forest.'
questions.explanation: 'Their' shows possession — the way belonging to the children. 'Their way' means the path they were following. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I went ___ the shops to buy some bread.'
questions.explanation: 'To' is a preposition showing direction or destination. 'I went to the shops' tells us where I went. 'Too' means 'also' or 'excessively', and 'two' is the number 2. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Can I come ___?'
questions.explanation: 'Too' means 'also' or 'as well'. 'Can I come too?' means 'Can I come as well?' ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We need ___ eggs for the recipe.'
questions.explanation: 'Two' is the number 2. 'We need two eggs' means we need the quantity of 2 eggs. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ the ball straight at the stumps.'
questions.explanation: 'Threw' is the past tense of 'throw'. 'She threw the ball' describes something that happened in the past. 'Through' means passing from one side to another. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The weather ___ affect whether the trip goes ahead.'
questions.explanation: 'Affect' is a verb meaning 'to influence'. 'The weather affects' — with the third person singular 's'. 'Effect' is usually a noun (the effect of something). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The ___ of the storm was felt across the whole village.'
questions.explanation: 'Effect' is a noun meaning 'result' or 'impact'. 'The effect of the storm' — we need a noun after 'the'. 'Affect' is a verb, not a noun. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ very kind of you to help me.'
questions.explanation: 'It's' is short for 'it is'. The sentence means 'It is very kind of you to help me.' ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I do not know ___ coat this is.'
questions.explanation: 'Whose' asks about possession — who does the coat belong to? 'Who's' means 'who is' or 'who has', which does not make sense here. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ coming to the party tonight?'
questions.explanation: 'Who's' is short for 'who is'. The sentence means 'Who is coming to the party tonight?' ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The team celebrated ___ victory with a special dinner.'
questions.explanation: 'Their' shows possession — the victory belonging to the team. 'Their victory' is correct. 'There' is a place and 'they're' means 'they are'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: '___ late for school if you do not hurry up.'
questions.explanation: 'You're' is short for 'you are'. The sentence means 'You are late for school.' 'Your' shows possession (your bag, your coat). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We walked ___ the village and ___ the forest beyond it.'
questions.explanation: 'Past' is a preposition meaning 'beyond' (walked past the village). 'Into' means 'to the inside of' (into the forest). 'Passed' is a verb (past tense of 'pass'), but here we need the preposition 'past'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The teacher gave us some good ___ about revising for the exam.'
questions.explanation: 'Advice' (with a 'c') is the noun — 'some good advice'. 'Advise' (with an 's') is the verb — 'I advise you to study'. In this sentence, we need the noun. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She sang ___  at the school concert.'
questions.explanation: We need an adverb to describe HOW she sang. 'Beautifully' is the adverb form of 'beautiful'. Adverbs modify verbs; adjectives modify nouns. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The tortoise moved ___ across the garden.'
questions.explanation: 'Slowly' is the adverb form, describing HOW the tortoise moved. 'Slow' is an adjective (a slow tortoise), but when describing a verb, we need the adverb. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He writes very ___.'
questions.explanation: 'Neatly' is the adverb form, describing HOW he writes. 'Neat' is an adjective that describes a noun (neat handwriting), but we need an adverb to describe the verb 'writes'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She felt ___ after eating too many sweets.'
questions.explanation: After 'felt', we use an adjective (not an adverb) because 'felt' is a linking verb describing her state. 'She felt bad' means she was unwell. 'Badly' would mean she was poor at the skill of feeling. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He did ___ in his maths test.'
questions.explanation: 'Well' is the adverb form of 'good'. We say 'he did well' (adverb describing how he did), not 'he did good'. 'Good' is an adjective and cannot modify a verb. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The soup tastes ___.'
questions.explanation: After linking verbs like 'tastes', 'looks', 'smells', and 'feels', we use an adjective, not an adverb. The adjective describes the soup: 'the soup tastes delicious'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The children played ___ in the playground.'
questions.explanation: 'Quietly' is the adverb form, describing HOW the children played. 'Quiet' is an adjective (a quiet game), but we need an adverb to describe the verb 'played'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The flowers smell ___.'
questions.explanation: After the linking verb 'smell', we use an adjective: 'the flowers smell sweet'. The adjective describes the flowers, not the action of smelling. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She could ___ believe what had happened.'
questions.explanation: 'Hardly' means 'almost not' or 'barely'. 'She could hardly believe it' means she found it very difficult to believe. Note: 'hardly' does not mean 'in a hard way' — it has a completely different meaning from 'hard'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Please speak ___ so that everyone can hear you.'
questions.explanation: 'Loudly' is the adverb form, describing HOW to speak. We need an adverb to modify the verb 'speak'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The door opened ___ and a cold breeze blew in.'
questions.explanation: 'Suddenly' is the adverb, describing HOW the door opened. Adverbs ending in '-ly' modify verbs. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She looked ___ when she heard the good news.'
questions.explanation: After the linking verb 'looked' (meaning 'appeared'), we use an adjective: 'she looked happy'. The adjective describes her appearance. If 'looked' meant 'gazed at', we would need an adverb. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The car drove ___ down the motorway.'
questions.explanation: 'Quickly' is the adverb form, describing HOW the car drove. 'Quick' is an adjective (a quick car), but we need an adverb to modify the verb 'drove'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'That was a very ___ thing to say.'
questions.explanation: Here we need an adjective to describe the noun 'thing'. 'A very kind thing' uses the adjective 'kind'. 'Kindly' is an adverb used to describe actions (she spoke kindly). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He worked ___ to finish the project on time.'
questions.explanation: 'Hard' is one of the unusual words that can be both an adjective AND an adverb without changing form. 'He worked hard' uses 'hard' as an adverb. 'Hardly' means 'barely' or 'almost not', which has a completely different meaning. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She answered all the questions ___.'
questions.explanation: 'Correctly' is the adverb form, describing HOW she answered. We need an adverb to modify the verb 'answered'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The teacher seemed ___ about the class's behaviour.'
questions.explanation: After the linking verb 'seemed', we use an adjective: 'seemed angry'. Linking verbs (be, seem, appear, feel, look) are followed by adjectives, not adverbs. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The train arrived ___.'
questions.explanation: 'Late' can be used as both an adjective and an adverb without changing form. 'The train arrived late' uses 'late' as an adverb. Note: 'lately' means 'recently', not 'in a late manner'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She danced ___ across the stage.'
questions.explanation: 'Gracefully' is the adverb form, describing HOW she danced. We add '-ly' to the adjective 'graceful' to make the adverb. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The cake looks ___ but tastes ___.'
questions.explanation: Both 'looks' and 'tastes' are linking verbs here, so both need adjectives: 'looks bad' and 'tastes good'. Linking verbs connect the subject to a description (adjective), not an action (adverb). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ like chocolate ice cream.'
questions.explanation: 'She' is a singular subject, so we use 'doesn't' (does not). 'Don't' (do not) is used with I, you, we, and they. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The children have been asked to ___ quietly in the hall.'
questions.explanation: After 'to', we need the infinitive (base form) of the verb: 'to sit'. 'Sat' is the past tense and 'sitting' is the present participle — neither follows 'to'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He could not decide ___ to go to the cinema or stay at home.'
questions.explanation: 'Whether' is used when presenting two alternatives: 'whether to do A or B'. 'If' is sometimes used informally, but 'whether' is more precise when there are two clear options. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We ___ play outside because it was raining.'
questions.explanation: 'Couldn't' (could not) expresses inability in the past. Because of the rain, they were unable to play outside. 'Could' is used for past ability, and the negative form 'couldn't' shows it was not possible. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She asked me ___ I had finished my homework.'
questions.explanation: 'If' is used in reported yes/no questions. The original question was 'Have you finished your homework?' — a yes/no question. When reported, it becomes 'She asked me if I had finished.' ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Despite ___ hard, he did not pass the exam.'
questions.explanation: After 'despite', we need a noun or gerund (-ing form). 'Despite studying hard' uses the gerund 'studying'. 'Despite' cannot be followed by a clause (subject + verb). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'There ___ lots of children at the party.'
questions.explanation: The real subject is 'lots of children' (plural), so we need a plural verb. 'Were' is the past plural form: 'there were lots of children'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He speaks French as ___ as his mother does.'
questions.explanation: 'Well' is the adverb form of 'good'. We say 'speaks well' because the adverb describes HOW he speaks. 'Good' is an adjective and cannot modify a verb. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Had I known about the problem, I ___ helped sooner.'
questions.explanation: 'Had I known...I would have helped' is the third conditional with inversion. 'Had I known' replaces 'If I had known' for a more formal style. The result clause uses 'would have + past participle'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Mum said ___ must tidy our rooms before going out.'
questions.explanation: 'We' is the subject pronoun — the subject of 'must tidy'. 'We must tidy our rooms' is correct. 'Us' is an object pronoun and cannot be the subject of a verb. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'This painting is different ___ the one in the other gallery.'
questions.explanation: In British English, 'different from' is the correct and most formal form. 'Different to' is sometimes used informally, and 'different than' is American English. For exams, use 'different from'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The more you practise, the ___ you will become.'
questions.explanation: 'The more...the better' is a comparative structure showing that two things increase together. As practice increases, skill improves. 'Better' is the comparative form of 'good'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We ___ going to the park after school.'
questions.explanation: 'We were' is correct Standard English. 'We was' is a common spoken error but grammatically wrong — 'was' is for singular subjects (I was, he was), while 'were' is for plural (we were, they were). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ her homework before tea yesterday.'
questions.explanation: 'She did' is correct Standard English. 'She done' is a very common spoken error — 'done' needs a helper verb ('has done', 'had done'), but on its own the past tense is 'did'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'They ___ playing in the garden when it started to rain.'
questions.explanation: 'They were' is correct. 'They was' is non-standard — 'was' is singular, 'were' is plural. With 'they', always use 'were'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I ___ seen that film before.'
questions.explanation: 'I have seen' is correct. With 'I', we always use 'have' not 'has'. 'Has' is for he/she/it: 'she has seen'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He ___ want to come to the party.'
questions.explanation: 'He doesn't' is correct Standard English. 'He don't' is a common spoken error — with he/she/it, we use 'doesn't', not 'don't'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We ___ allowed to run in the corridors.'
questions.explanation: 'We aren't' is correct. 'We' is plural so needs 'aren't', not 'isn't' (which is singular). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'My friends and I ___ to the cinema last Saturday.'
questions.explanation: 'Went' is the correct past tense. 'We gone' is a common spoken error — 'gone' needs a helper verb ('have gone'), but on its own the past tense of 'go' is 'went'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ never been to Scotland before.'
questions.explanation: 'She has' is correct. With she/he/it, we use 'has' not 'have'. 'She have' is non-standard English. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I ___ it on the table this morning.'
questions.explanation: 'I saw' is the correct past tense. 'I seen' is a very common spoken error — 'seen' needs a helper verb ('I have seen'), but on its own the past tense of 'see' is 'saw'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Mum ___ us to tidy our rooms.'
questions.explanation: 'Told' is the correct past tense of 'tell'. 'Telled' and 'telt' are non-standard forms. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The children ___ very excited about the school trip.'
questions.explanation: 'The children were' is correct. 'Children' is plural, so it takes 'were' not 'was'. 'The children was' is non-standard. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He ___ his coat on the floor again.'
questions.explanation: 'Left' is the correct past tense of 'leave'. 'Leaved' is a common error — 'leave' is irregular: leave/left/left. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She should ___ told me about the change of plan.'
questions.explanation: 'Should have' is correct. 'Should of' is a very common error because 'should've' sounds like 'should of' when spoken aloud. But 'of' is a preposition, not a verb — it must be 'have'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'If I ___ known about the party, I would have come.'
questions.explanation: 'If I had known' is the third conditional, used for imaginary past situations. 'If I would known' and 'If I have known' are both grammatically wrong. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We could ___ left earlier if you had been ready.'
questions.explanation: 'Could have' is correct. Like 'should have', 'could of' is a common error caused by the contraction 'could've' sounding like 'could of'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'My sister and ___ went shopping on Saturday.'
questions.explanation: 'My sister and I' is correct because 'I' is the subject (doing the action). Test by removing 'my sister and' — you'd say 'I went shopping', not 'me went shopping'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The teacher told my brother and ___ to stay behind.'
questions.explanation: 'My brother and me' is correct because 'me' is the object (receiving the action). Test by removing 'my brother and' — you'd say 'told me', not 'told I'. Many people overcorrect to 'I' here. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He might ___ forgotten about the meeting.'
questions.explanation: 'Might have' is correct. 'Might of' is wrong — same error as 'should of' and 'could of'. The contracted form 'might've' sounds like 'might of' but must be written as 'might have'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Those cakes ___ delicious.'
questions.explanation: 'Those cakes taste' is correct. 'Those' is plural, so needs a plural verb. 'Tastes' is singular ('the cake tastes'), 'taste' is plural ('the cakes taste'). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ to school every day last term.'
questions.explanation: 'Walked' is correct — the past tense is needed because of 'last term'. 'She walks' is present tense, 'has walked' is present perfect — neither fits the past time reference. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I ___ rather go to the park than stay indoors.'
questions.explanation: 'I would rather' is the correct phrase for expressing preference. 'I will rather' and 'I shall rather' are non-standard. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'You ___ to ask before borrowing someone's things.'
questions.explanation: 'You ought to ask' is correct. 'Ought' must always be followed by 'to'. 'You should ask' would also be correct but 'should' isn't paired with 'to' the same way. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'There ___ fewer pupils in school today because of the flu.'
questions.explanation: 'There are fewer' is correct. 'Fewer' is used with countable nouns (pupils), and 'pupils' is plural, so we need 'are'. Also note: 'fewer' not 'less' with countable nouns. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ well in her exams this year.'
questions.explanation: 'She has done well' is correct. 'She done well' is non-standard — 'done' always needs a helper verb. 'She did well' would also be correct in past simple, but the 'this year' suggests the present perfect. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'None of the children ___ finished their work on time.'
questions.explanation: 'None has finished' is traditionally correct — 'none' means 'not one' and takes a singular verb. However, 'none have' is widely accepted too. In formal/exam English, singular is preferred. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'He ran ___ than anyone else in the race.'
questions.explanation: 'More quickly' is correct. We need an adverb (describing HOW he ran), not an adjective. 'Quicker' is informal but technically an adjective. In Standard English, 'more quickly' is the correct comparative adverb. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Mum asked ___ had eaten the last biscuit.'
questions.explanation: 'Who' is correct because it's the subject of 'had eaten' (who did the eating?). 'Whom' would be wrong here — 'whom' is for the object position. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'We ___ to the new restaurant twice already.'
questions.explanation: 'We have been' is correct. 'We been' is non-standard — the helper verb 'have' is needed. 'Already' signals the present perfect tense. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Between you and ___, I think the test was quite easy.'
questions.explanation: 'Between you and me' is correct. 'Between' is a preposition, so it takes the object form 'me', not the subject form 'I'. 'Between you and I' is a very common hypercorrection — people think 'I' always sounds more formal, but after a preposition, 'me' is correct. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'If I ___ you, I would apologise immediately.'
questions.explanation: This is the subjunctive mood, used for imaginary situations. Even with 'I', we use 'were' (not 'was') in 'if' clauses about things that aren't true: 'If I were you...' This is formal Standard English. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The headteacher requested that every pupil ___ present at the assembly.'
questions.explanation: This is the formal subjunctive after verbs of requesting/demanding. 'Be' is the subjunctive form — 'The headteacher requested that every pupil be present.' This is very formal English, typically D3 difficulty. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'To ___ should I address this complaint?'
questions.explanation: 'To whom' is correct. After a preposition ('to'), we use 'whom' not 'who'. Test: you'd say 'I should address it to him' (object), not 'I should address it to he' (subject), so it's 'whom'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'It is essential that she ___ on time for the examination.'
questions.explanation: 'Arrive' is the subjunctive form after 'it is essential that'. In the subjunctive, we use the base form of the verb regardless of the subject: 'that she arrive', 'that he attend', 'that it be'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She asked ___ had left the gate open.'
questions.explanation: 'Who' is correct because it's the subject of 'had left' — who did the leaving? 'Whom' would be wrong here. Many people overcorrect to 'whom' thinking it sounds more formal, but 'who' is the subject form. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The prize was given to my friend and ___.'
questions.explanation: 'My friend and me' is correct. 'To' is a preposition, so takes the object form 'me'. Test by removing 'my friend and' — you'd say 'given to me', not 'given to I'. This is one of the most common hypercorrections. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She speaks ___ she were an expert on the subject.'
questions.explanation: 'As if' is correct for making a comparison with an imaginary situation. 'Like' is used informally but is not Standard English in this context — 'as if' or 'as though' is the correct formal form. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The teacher, along with three teaching assistants, ___ supervising the trip.'
questions.explanation: 'Is' is correct. The subject is 'the teacher' (singular). 'Along with three teaching assistants' is additional information but doesn't change the subject. This is a common GL trap — the nearby plural 'assistants' attracts children to pick 'are'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Had I ___ about the problem earlier, I could have helped.'
questions.explanation: 'Had I known' is an inverted conditional (formal version of 'If I had known'). The past participle 'known' is needed after 'had'. This formal inversion drops 'if' and puts 'had' first. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Not only ___ she finish first, but she also broke the record.'
questions.explanation: 'Not only did she finish' uses inverted word order with 'did' for emphasis. After 'not only', the auxiliary verb comes before the subject: 'did she', not 'she did'. This is formal English. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Scarcely had the lesson begun ___ the fire alarm sounded.'
questions.explanation: 'Scarcely...when' is the correct pairing. Compare: 'No sooner...than' and 'Hardly...when'. These formal structures use specific conjunctions — mixing them up ('scarcely...than') is a common error. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The basket of oranges ___ sitting on the kitchen table.'
questions.explanation: The subject is 'basket' (singular), not 'oranges'. The phrase 'of oranges' just describes what's in the basket. A singular subject takes 'is'. Don't be distracted by the nearby plural noun! ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'One of my friends ___ moving to a new school next term.'
questions.explanation: The subject is 'one' (singular), not 'friends'. 'Of my friends' tells us which one. Since 'one' is singular, we need 'is'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The packet of crisps ___ already been opened.'
questions.explanation: The subject is 'packet' (singular). 'Of crisps' just describes the packet. 'Has' is the singular form needed here. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The number of pupils arriving by bus ___ increased this year.'
questions.explanation: 'The number of' is singular — it refers to the number itself. So 'has increased' is correct. Compare with 'A number of pupils have...' where 'a number of' means 'many' and takes a plural verb. This is a classic GL distinction! ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'A number of children ___ absent today because of illness.'
questions.explanation: 'A number of' means 'several/many' and takes a plural verb: 'are absent'. Compare with 'The number of children is...' where 'the number' is singular. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Every pupil in the school ___ expected to wear uniform.'
questions.explanation: 'Every' means each individual one, so it takes a singular verb: 'is expected'. Even though there are many pupils, 'every' treats them individually. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Neither the captain nor the players ___ satisfied with the result.'
questions.explanation: With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the nearest noun. 'Players' is nearest and is plural, so we need 'were'. If it were 'Neither the players nor the captain', we'd use 'was'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Either the twins or their older sister ___ responsible for feeding the cat.'
questions.explanation: With 'either...or', the verb agrees with the nearest noun. 'Sister' is nearest and is singular, so we need 'is'. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'Bread and butter ___ my favourite snack.'
questions.explanation: 'Bread and butter' is treated as a single item (one snack), so takes the singular 'is'. When two nouns form a single concept ('fish and chips is...', 'salt and pepper is...'), use a singular verb. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'The jury ___ unable to reach a unanimous verdict.'
questions.explanation: 'Were' is correct here because the jury members couldn't agree — they're acting as individuals, not as one unit. When a collective noun's members act separately, use a plural verb. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'By the time we reached the cinema, the film had already ___.'
questions.explanation: 'Had started' is the past perfect tense, showing one past event happened before another. The film started (first event) before we reached the cinema (second event). ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'She ___ the violin for three hours when her teacher finally arrived.'
questions.explanation: 'Had been practising' is the past perfect progressive, used for an ongoing action that was happening up to a point in the past. She was continuously practising (duration: three hours) before the teacher arrived. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'I ___ never tasted mango before I went to India.'
questions.explanation: 'Had' is correct — the past perfect ('had tasted') is needed because there are two past events: tasting mango and going to India. The tasting (or lack of it) happened before going to India. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'By next September, she ___ completed all her 11+ preparation.'
questions.explanation: 'Will have completed' is the future perfect tense, used for something that will be finished before a specific future time. 'By next September' signals that future deadline. ✓

questions.question: Choose the correct word to complete the sentence: 'While Dad ___ the car, it suddenly started to rain.'
questions.explanation: 'Was washing' is the past progressive (continuous), used for an ongoing action that was interrupted. Dad's washing was in progress when the rain interrupted it. 'Washed' would suggest he finished. ✓
